---
title: "Benchmark to Bedside"
subtitle: 'Building Modular Multimodal Machine Learning Infrastructure for Healthcare at Scale   <br>
 <img src="figures/slides_link_trans.svg" style="height:5em;vertical-align:middle;"> <br> <span style="font-size:0.5em;">Scan for interactive slides</span> <br> <img src="images/logos/logos_defense.svg" width="30%">  '
author: 'Robin P. van de Water'
date: today
date-format: "DD MMMM YYYY"
bibliography: bibliography.bib
csl: brief.csl               # <-- Use a numeric CSL file
filters:
  - footnote-letters.lua
  - editable
format:
  revealjs:
   theme: serif
  #  transition: fade
   slide-number: true
   fontsize: 1.5em
   chalkboard: false
   preview-links: auto
   logo: ""
   css: custom.css
   footnotes-hover: true
   footer: rpvandewater.com/dissertation-defense/ | PhD Defense | Robin P. van de Water
   footnotes: true
   simplemenu:
   code-overflow: wrap
revealjs-plugins:
  - editable
#   - simplemenu

--- 

## ML for Health in a Nutshell
<!-- ![](figures/motivation/ml_for_health.svg){fig-cap="World Health Organization" height=25em .no-dark} -->
<div style="position: relative; display: inline-block;">
  <img src="figures/motivation/ml_for_health.svg" style="height:25em; display:block;" class="no-dark">

  <div class="fragment fade-out" data-fragment-index="1"
       style="position:absolute; top:0%; left:15%; width:100%; height:100%; background:white; z-index:2;"></div>

  <div class="fragment fade-out" data-fragment-index="2"
       style="position:absolute; top:0; left:55%; width:100%; height:100%; background:white; z-index:2;"></div>
</div>

::: {.notes}
- Hospitals, clinics, and healthcare systems generate vast amounts of data, including electronic health records (EHRs), medical imaging, and physiological signals.
- Machine learning can be used on parts of this data to predict patient outcomes, assist in diagnosis, and optimize treatment plans.
- The essence is often predicting or diagnosing something about a patient at time T so that we can intervene at time T+1 to improve outcomes.
:::

---

## Electronic Health Records (EHR): Footprints of the Patient Journey

![](figures/motivation/ehr_simplified.svg){fig-cap="EHR Data Structure" width=80%}

::: {.notes}
What does EHR data look like? 
- It's a complex, heterogeneous, and often messy collection of structured codes (diagnoses, labs, medications) and unstructured text (clinical notes). 
- This complexity is both a challenge and an opportunity for machine learning in healthcare. 
- We should distinguish between in-patient and out-patient data, the latter is much more sparse and less frequently updated, which has implications for model design and deployment. 
- The richness of EHR data allows for powerful predictive models, but also requires careful handling to ensure reproducibility and clinical relevance.
:::

---

## The Promise of Machine Learning in Healthcare
Papers on ArXiv preprint server with ML and health keywords in the title or abstract have been growing over the last years:
![](figures/motivation/ml_health_trend.svg){fig-cap="EHR Data Structure" width=100%}

``` python
ML_PATTERN = r"(?ix)\b(machine[ _]?learning|deep[ _]?learning|neural[ _]?networks?|artificial[ _]?intelligence|\bAI\b|random[ _]?forest|support[ _]?vector[ _]?(machine|regression|classif\w+)?|\bSVM\b|gradient[ _]?boost\w*|\bXGBoost\b|\bLightGBM\b|\bCatBoost\b|\btransformer\b|\bBERT\b|\bGPT\b|\bLLM\b|\bVLM\b|large[ _]?language[ _]?models?|foundation[ _]?models?|(convolutional|recurrent|graph)[ _]?neural|\bLSTM\b|\bGRU\b|generative[ _]?adversarial|\bGAN\b|reinforcement[ _]?learning|(semi|self|un)[ _-]?supervised[ _]?learning|transfer[ _]?learning|federated[ _]?learning|natural[ _]?language[ _]?processing|\bNLP\b|computer[ _]?vision|(image|object|text)[ _]?(classification|detection|segmentation)|\bautoencoder\b|\bdiffusion[ _]?model\b)\b"
HEALTH_PATTERN = r"(?ix)\b(health(care)?|medical|clinical|patients?|hospital|diseases?|disorders?|syndrome|diagnosis|diagnostics?|prognosis|prognostic|treatments?|therapy|therapeutic|\bEHR\b|\bEMR\b|epidemiol\w*|radiol\w*|pathol\w*|genomics?|proteomics?|transcriptomics?|biomarkers?|\bdrug\b|pharmaceutical|pharmacolog\w*|cancer|tumor|tumour|oncol\w*|cardiac|cardiovascular|coronary|diabetes|diabetic|\bCOVID\b|\bSARS\b|pandemic|epidemic|biomedical|mortality|morbidity|\bICU\b|intensive[ _]?care|surgical|surgery|symptoms?|comorbid\w*|screening|triage|mental[ _]?health|psychiatr\w*|neurolog\w*|\bMRI\b|\bCT[ _]?scan\b)\b"

```
---

## The Translation Gap in Healthcare {auto-animate="true"}

::: {.incremental}
- The problem statement motivating this work: **Despite the promise and explosion of machine learning research, few models have been successfully deployed in clinical practice.** ^[[@apellExplainingSlowAdoption2026] [@henzlerHealthcareProfessionalsPerspectives2025]]
- Why?
  - ​⚡​ **Research reproducibility**: Different papers report different performance for the same task due to variations in datasets, preprocessing, and evaluation.^[[@futomaMythGeneralisabilityClinical2020]]
  - ​⚡​ **Incomplete observations**: Retrospective (administrative) data does not necessarily reflect the information needed for clinical decision-making. ^[[@agnielBiasesElectronicHealth2018]]
  - ​⚡​ **Lack of robust infrastructure**: Each institution builds their own custom solutions, making it hard to share research results and validate across sites.^[[@mcdermottLackScienceMachine2025] [@donohoDataScienceSingularity2024]]
  - ​⚡​ **Bonus: Ethical and regulatory hurdles**: Ensuring patient privacy, fairness, and clinical utility to authorities is non-trivial.^[[@vancalsterThereNoSuch2023]]
:::

::: {.notes}
- The translation gap is due to a combination of factors:
  - Reproducibility: Addressed with the YAIB framework, which standardizes benchmarking across datasets and preprocessing choices.
  - Incomplete observations: Addressed with the CASSANDRA study, which prospectively collects data and validates models in real clinical settings.
  - Lack of infrastructure: Addressed with the MEDS framework, which provides a standardized data format and tools for scalable research across institutions.
  - Ethical and regulatory hurdles: Depending on the context, these are addressed through careful study design, data governance, and transparency in reporting.
:::
<!-- > "Despite significant advances in machine learning capabilities, few AI systems have been successfully deployed into clinical practice. Key barriers include reproducibility challenges, generalization failures, and lack of standardized evaluation frameworks." -->

<!-- :::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Current State:**
- Limited open-source tools
- Dataset-specific implementations
- Proprietary data formats
- Few external validations
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Consequences:**
- Non-reproducible results
- Slow scientific progress
- Inefficient use of clinical data
- Barriers to adoption
:::
:::: -->
---

## Research Contribution per Challenge {auto-animate=true}

::: {.bigger-text}

::: {.incremental}
1. ​⚡​ Research reproducibility → <span data-id="repro-phrase">Enabling Reproducible Prediction Experiments 🧪</span>  ✓
2. ​⚡​ Incomplete observations → <span data-id="incomplete-phrase">Development and Validation of Multi-ward Multi-modal Early Warning Systems 🛏️​</span> 
3. ​⚡​ Lack of infrastructure → <span data-id="infrastructure-phrase">Packages and pipelines for Foundational Health AI Research 🏗️</span> 
:::
::: {.fragment .fade-in}
**Thesis Statement:** *ML systems for health care require rich data, reusable open-source model components, and an extensive validation ecosystem.*
:::

:::

::: {.notes}
Add icons here for each contribution, and maybe a visual overview of the contributions and how they relate to each other.

My thesis is structured around three main contributions that address the key challenges in translating machine learning research into clinical practice.
1. Reproducible Prediction Experiments: I developed the YAIB framework to enable reproducible benchmarking of ICU prediction models across different datasets and preprocessing choices. This helps us understand why different papers report different performance for the same clinical task.
2. Development of Applied Multi-ward Multi-modal Early Warning Systems: I conducted the CASSANDRA study, which prospectively collects data from surgical patients in the general ward and integrates wearable device data to improve early warning systems for postoperative complications.
3. Packages and pipelines for Foundational Health AI Research: I contributed to the development of the MEDS framework, which provides a standardized data format and tools for scalable machine learning research across healthcare institutions, enabling better reproducibility and collaboration.
4. Thesis Statement: The overarching thesis is that healthcare machine learning requires rich data, reusable model components, and an extensive validation ecosystem to bridge the gap between research and clinical practice.
:::
---

## Research Pillars Contributions {auto-animate=true}

:::: columns


::: {.column width="33%"}
<span data-id="repro-phrase">Enabling Reproducible Prediction Experiments 🧪</span> 

::: {.tiny-text}
- **Robin P. van de Water**, Hendrik Schmidt, and Patrick Rockenschaub. "ReciPies: A Lightweight Data Transformation Pipeline for Reproducible ML". in: *Journal of Open Source Software* 11.117 (2026), p. 9261. doi: 10.21105/joss.09261 [@vandewaterReciPiesLightweightData2026]
- **Robin van de Water**, Hendrik Nils Aurel Schmidt, Paul Elbers, Patrick Thoral, Bert Arnrich, and Patrick Rockenschaub. "Yet Another ICU Benchmark: A Flexible Multi-Center Framework for Clinical ML". in: *Proceedings of The Twelfth International Conference on Learning Representations.* May 2024. doi: 10.48550/arXiv.2306.05109. [@vandewaterAnotherICUBenchmark2024a]
:::
:::

::: {.column width="33%"}
<span data-id="incomplete-phrase">Development and Validation of Multi-ward Multi-modal Early Warning Systems 🛏️​</span>

::: {.tiny-text}
- **Robin P. van de Water**, Axel Winter, Daniela Zuluaga Lotero, Bjarne Pfitzner, Lara Faraj, Bert Arnrich, Patrick Rockenschaub, Wenzel Schoning, Thomas Malinka, Christian Denecke, Johann Pratschke, Igor M. Sauer, and Max M. Maurer. Continuous Multimodal AI with Wearable Vital Signs Predicts Postoperative Complications in the General Ward. Nov. 2025. doi: 10.1101/2025.11.25.25340950. [@vandewaterContinuousMultimodalAI2025] (MedRxiv preprint)
- Christoph Riepe, **Robin van de Water**, Axel Winter, Bjarne Pfitzner, Lara Faraj, Robert Ahlborn, Maximilian Schulze, Daniela Zuluaga, Christian Schineis, Katharina Beyer, Johann Pratschke, Bert Arnrich, Igor M. Sauer, and Max M. Maurer. "90-Day Mortality Prediction in Elective Visceral Surgery Using Machine Learning: A Retrospective Multicenter Development, Validation, and Comparison Study". In: *International Journal of Surgery* 111.6 (June 2025), p. 3742. doi: 10.1097/JS9.0000000000002372. [@riepe90dayMortalityPrediction2025a]
- **Robin van de Water**, Axel Winter, Max M. Maurer, Felix August Treykorn, Daniela Zuluaga, Bjarne Pfitzner, Igor M. Sauer, and Bert Arnrich. "Combining Hospital-grade Clinical Data and Wearable Vital Sign Monitoring to Predict Surgical Complications". In: *ICLR 2024 Workshop on Learning from Time Series For Health.* Mar. 2024. [@waterCombiningHospitalgradeClinical2024b]
- **Robin van de Water**, Axel Winter, Max M. Maurer, Felix August Treykorn, Bjarne Pfitzner, Igor M. Sauer, and Bert Arnrich. "Combining Time Series Modalities to Create Endpoint-driven Patient Records". In: *ICLR 2024 Workshop on Data-centric Machine Learning Research (DMLR): Harnessing Momentum for Science.* Mar. 2024. [@waterCombiningTimeSeries2024] 
:::
:::
::: {.column width="33%"}
<span data-id="infrastructure-phrase">Packages and pipelines for Foundational Health AI Research 🏗️</span>

::: {.tiny-text}
- **MEDS Working Group**: (alphabetized) Bert Arnrich, Edward Choi, Jason A. Fries, Matthew B. A. McDermott, Jungwoo Oh, Tom J. Pollard, Nigam Shah, Ethan Steinberg, Michael Wornow, **Robin van de Water**. "Medical Event Data Standard (MEDS): Facilitating Machine Learning for Health". In: *ICLR 2024 Workshop on Learning from Time Series for Health*. 2024. [@medsworkinggroupMedicalEventData2024]
- Matthew B. A. McDermott, Aleksia Kolo, Chao Pang, Edward Choi, Ethan Steinberg, Hyewon Jeong, Jack Gallifant, Jason Alan Fries, Jeffrey N. Chiang, Jungwoo Oh, Justin Xu, Kamilė Stankevičiūtė, Kiril Vadimovic Klein, Mikkel Fruelund Odgaard, Nassim Oufattole, Patrick Rockenschaub, Pawel Renc, **Robin van de Water**, Shalmali Joshi, Simon Austin Lee, Teya Bergamaschi, Tom Pollard, Vincent Jeanselme, Nigam Shah, Michael Wornow, Aparajita Kashyap, Xinzhou Jiang, Yanwei Li, Young Sang Choi, Yuta Kobayashi, and Ryan King. "MEDS Decentralized, Extensible Validation (MEDS-DEV) Benchmark: Establishing Reproducibility and Comparability in ML for Health". In: *Machine Learning for Health (ML4H)*, 15-16 December 2024, Vancouver, Canada (Nov. 2024). [@mcdermottMEDSDecentralizedExtensible2024]
- Matthew B. A. McDermott, Justin Xu, Teya S. Bergamaschi, Hyewon Jeong, Simon A. Lee, Nassim Oufattole, Patrick Rockenschaub, Kamilė Stankevičiūtė, Ethan Steinberg, Jimeng Sun, **Robin P. Van De Water**, Michael Wornow, John Wu, and Zhenbang Wu. "MEDS: Building Models and Tools in a Reproducible Health AI Ecosystem". In: *Proceedings of the 31st ACM SIGKDD Conference on Knowledge Discovery and Data Mining* V.2. Toronto ON Canada: ACM, Aug. 2025, pp. 6243–6244. isbn: 979-8-4007-1454-2. doi: 10.1145/3711896.3737608. [@mcdermottMEDSBuildingModels2025a]
- **Robin Philippus van de Water**, Ethan Steinberg, Michael Wornow, Patrick Rockenschaub, and Matthew McDermott. MIMIC-IV Demo Data in the Medical Event Data Standard (MEDS). 2025. doi: 10.13026/T2Y8-EA41. [@vandewaterMIMICIVDemoData2025]
- **Robin P. Van de Water**. "Scaling Up Clinical ML from Datasets to Entire Health Systems through the MEDS Ecosystem". In: *SBHD 2025 International Conference on Systems Biology of Human Diseases*. Berlin, June 2025 [@vandewaterScalingClinicalML2025]
:::
:::

::::

{{< include extra_slides/publications.qmd >}}

<!-- **PhD Research Focus:** -->

<!-- The translation of machine learning from research to clinical practice remains fundamentally challenged by three interconnected problems: -->
<!-- 
:::: columns
::: {.column width="33%"}
**Reproducibility**
How do we ensure models perform consistently across different datasets and institutions?
:::
::: {.column width="33%"}
**Clinical Deployment**
How do we build prediction systems that actually work in messy real-world clinical settings?
:::
::: {.column width="33%"}
**Scalable Infrastructure**
How do we enable the entire community to build on each other's work?
:::
:::: -->


# Establishing Reproducibility in Open-access EHR Prediction Modeling



## ​⚡The Reproducibility Crisis in Clinical ML

**Problem:** ​⚡​ Comparing ML approaches across papers is nearly impossible because of different:

-  Datasets (MIMIC-III vs MIMIC-IV vs eICU vs HiRID)
-  Cohort definitions (patient inclusion/exclusion varies)
-  Preprocessing (normalization, imputation, features)
-  Evaluation metrics and splits
But mostly:
- Code not published, incomplete, or not reusable

Our conclusion:
*Dataset, cohort definition, and preprocessing choices have **larger effects on performance** than the choice of model architecture.*

---

## Our answer

![](figures/yaib/YAIB_logo.svg){fig-cap="YAIB Logo" width=80%}

---


## Yet Another ICU Benchmark (YAIB)

::: {.incremental}
Problems:

- Medical research is **inherently** complex
- One size fits-all always **impossible**
- Hardcoded ≠ **reproducible**


Goals:

- **Modular** setup maintaining reproducibility
- **Extensibility** across experiment lifecycle
- **Out-of-the-box** support: datasets, tasks, models

Result: 

- ReciPies: Modular ML preprocessing framework ^[[@vandewaterReciPiesLightweightData2026]]
- YAIB Framework^[[@vandewaterAnotherICUBenchmark2024a]]: enables reproducible comparisons while remaining flexible enough to accommodate real-world research needs.
:::
<!-- ::: {.incremental}
1. **Data Harmonization** [@Ben23]
2. **Modular Preprocessing** (ReciPies) [@vSR26]
3. **Standardized, explicit, configurable evaluation** [@DGS18]
::: -->

---

## The YAIB Experimental Pipeline



<div style="text-align: center;">
![](figures/yaib/yaib_flow_combined.svg){fig-cap="YAIB Experimental Pipeline" width=100%}
</div>

---

## ReciPies: A Simple Modular Preprocessing Framework for ML Research
![](figures/yaib/recipies_flow.svg){fig-cap="ReciPies Flow" width=100% .no-dark}

<div style="position: absolute; bottom: 0; right: 0; text-align: right;">
![](figures/yaib/recipies_logo.svg){fig-cap="My caption" width=10% .no-dark}
</div>
---
 
## Datasets and Tasks

Four publicly available ICU datasets:

<!-- - MIMIC-IV^[[@johnsonMIMICIVFreelyAccessible2023]]
- eICU^[[@pollardEICUCollaborativeResearch2018]]
- HiRID^[[@hylandEarlyPredictionCirculatory2020]]
- AUMCdb^[[@thoralSharingICUPatient2021]] -->
| **Dataset**                  | **MIMIC-III / IV** | **eICU** | **HiRID** | **AUMCdb** |
|------------------------------|--------------------|----------|-----------|------------|
| **Stays**                  | 40k (0.1k) / 73k | 201k (2k) | 34k       | 23k        |
| **Version**                 | v1.4 / v2.2        | v2.0     | v1.1.1    | v1.0.2     |
| **Frequency (time-series)** | 1 hour             | 5 minutes| 2 / 5 minutes | up to 1 minute |
| **Origin**                  | USA                | USA      | Switzerland | Netherlands |
| **Originally published**    | 2015^[[@johnsonMIMICIIIFreelyAccessible2016]] / 2020^[[@johnsonMIMICIVFreelyAccessible2023]] | 2017^[[@pollardEICUCollaborativeResearch2018]] | 2020^[[@hylandEarlyPredictionCirculatory2020]] | 2019^[[@thoralSharingICUPatient2021]] |

**Five common ICU prediction tasks**: Mortality, Acute Kidney Injury, Sepsis, Kidney Function, Length of Stay.

---

## Sepsis: Different Definitions, Different Conclusions
::: {.incremental}


<!-- | **Algorithm** | \multicolumn{2}{c}{Seymour et al.^[[@seymourAssessmentClinicalCriteria2016]]*} | \multicolumn{2}{c}{Moor et al. ^[[@moorPredictingSepsisMultisite2021]]} | \multicolumn{2}{c}{Calvert et al. ^[[@calvertComputationalApproachEarly2016]]} -->

| **Algorithm** | Seymour et al.^[[@seymourAssessmentClinicalCriteria2016]]* |         | Moor et al. ^[[@moorPredictingSepsisMultisite2021]] |         | Calvert et al. ^[[@calvertComputationalApproachEarly2016]] |         |
|---------------|-----------------|---------|-----------------|---------|-----------------|---------|
|               | **AUROC**       | **AUPRC** | **AUROC**       | **AUPRC** | **AUROC**       | **AUPRC** |
| **LGBM**      | 75.9±0.2        | 4.3±0.0  | 72.4±0.0        | 10.5±0.0 | 62.2±0.2        | 1.8±0.0  |
| **GRU**       | **79.2±0.1**    | **6.1±0.0** | **80.9±0.0**    | **17.7±0.0** | **89.2±0.0**    | **9.3±0.2** |

- Sepsis definition can significantly alter the resulting Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve (AUROC) and Area Under the Precision-Recall Curve (AUPRC)
- Large differences of inclusion in cohort
:::

::: {.notes}
**Note:** Our definition (marked with *), adapted to be more clinically actionable. See Appendix for details.
:::
---

## External Validation with Mortality

::: {.column width="50%"}
AUROC:
![](figures/yaib/ext_val_mortality_pooled_auroc.svg){fig-cap="ReciPies Flow" width=100% .no-dark}

:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
AUPRC:
![](figures/yaib/ext_val_mortality_pooled_auprc.svg){fig-cap="ReciPies Flow" width=100% .no-dark}

:::
- **Column**: Model trained on dataset
- **Row**: Model evaluated on dataset
- **Diagonal**: Internal validation (same dataset)

---

## Transfer Learning

- What if we train a generalized model on "most generalizable" eICU?
- Finetune on "difficult" HiRID

::: {.column width="50%"}
AUROC:
![](figures/yaib/transfer_learning.svg){fig-cap="" width=100% .no-dark}

:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
AUPRC:
![](figures/yaib/transfer_learning_auprc.svg){fig-cap="" width=100% .no-dark}

:::
---

## Continuous AI-Assisted Wearable Monitoring for Early Detection of Postoperative Complications: A Prospective Cohort Study
::: {.incremental}
- More than 300 million surgical procedures performed annually worldwide^[[@dobsonTraumaMajorSurgery2020]]
- 1/3 of hospital costs are surgery-related^[[@shrimeGlobalBurdenSurgical2015]]
- Postoperative adverse events impact consequent quality of life, healthcare costs, and mortality^[[@brownImpactPostoperativeComplications2014; @downeyImpactInhospitalPostoperative2023; @kongASOVisualAbstract2021; @muallaImpactPostoperativeComplications2021; @sartelliComplicatedIntraabdominalInfections2014; @vonlanthenImpactComplicationsCosts2011; @wangPostoperativeComplicationsPrognosis2019]]
-  Problem: physiological patterns are often subtle, nonspecific, and time-varying, with substantial heterogeneity between patients^[[@roppeltArtificialIntelligenceHealthcare2024]]
:::

::: {.notes}
- Surgical patients are stabilized on the ICU, and then transferred to the general ward, where they are monitored less frequently.
- Postoperative complications are common and often go undetected until they become severe, leading to worse outcomes and higher costs.
:::

---

## Why not just monitor patients in the ICU?

::: {.incremental}
- ICU AI model research is booming^[[@hylandEarlyPredictionCirculatory2020a; @nematiInterpretableMachineLearning2018]], but:
  - ICU costs are high, recovery slow(er)
- Nursing ward is less resource constrained, but:
  - Severely less monitored
- Solution? Hybrid ward with minimally intrusive wearables
:::

::: {.notes}
- So why not just monitor patients in the ICU?
- ICU stays are expensive and can lead to complications like infections, delirium, and deconditioning.
:::

---

# A Prospective Cohort Study

::: {.column width="80%"}
![](figures/cass/wards.png){fig-cap="" width=100% .no-dark}
:::

::: {.column width="20%"}
![](figures/cass/greenteg_core.jpg){fig-cap="" width=100% .no-dark}
![](figures/cass/corsano.png){fig-cap="" width=100% .no-dark}
:::

---

## Introducing Clinical ASsist Heuristic Early Warning System (CASHEWS)

::: {.editable}
<!-- <div style="text-align: center;">
  <img src="figures/cass/fig1.svg" width="75%" class="no-dark">
</div> -->

![](figures/cass/fig1.svg){fig-cap="" width=100% .no-dark}

![](figures/cass/cashews_logo.svg){fig-cap="" width=100% .no-dark}

---
:::

::: {.notes}
Fig. 1: Data segmentation, aggregation, model training, and alarm framework.
a, Timeline of the perioperative patient journey in surgical patients. b, Prospective data curation by endpoint labeling and data extraction from hospital data information systems. c, Schematic overview of data extraction, domain-specific preprocessing, and temporal aggregation. Structured clinical data comprising static, categorical, and binary variables (e.g., demographic data, comorbidities, preoperative laboratory values) and dynamic time-varying measurements (e.g., repeated laboratory examinations, intensive care unit (ICU) vital signs) were processed using domain-specific preprocessing pipelines. Unstructured clinical text data, including clinical notes, medication orders, and fluid administration records, were embedded using a pre-trained multilingual JinAI model²⁷, from which standard natural language processing (NLP) features were derived. High-resolution physiological signals derived from patient wearables on the general ward, specifically photoplethysmography (PPG) and continuous body temperature measurement, were processed using the MINIROCKET embedding model. Heart rate variability (HRV) features were extracted from both time- and frequency-domain representations. Missing values were imputed, and statistical descriptors—including skewness, entropy, standard deviation, minimum, and maximum—were computed. To capture temporal dependencies, features from preceding time steps were incorporated. All data streams were aggregated into 30-minute time segments, each containing 7,280 distinct model features. Multi-modal feature fusion and endpoint alignment resulted in segmented clinical trajectories evaluated at a 9-hour prediction horizon. d, Multiple model architectures, encompassing classical machine learning (ML) and Deep Learning (DL), were trained and evaluated using cross-validation and hyperparameter optimization. Model selection was based on predictive performance and computational efficiency, with LightGBM identified as the most favorable trade-off. e, The optimal model was evaluated by performing domain ablation and feature importance analysis with Shapley explanations. f, Based upon continuous model outputs, an actional alarm system including specific alarming frameworks was evaluated. The slope coefficient and persistence quantify the increase in the probability of sounding an alarm. An 8-hour silencing period is implemented to prevent alarm fatigue.
:::
---

::: {.editable}
<!-- <div style="text-align: center;">
  <img src="figures/cass/fig1.svg" width="75%" class="no-dark">
</div> -->

![](figures/cass/fig2.svg){fig-cap="" width=100% .no-dark}

:::
::: {.notes}

:::
---

::: {.editable}
<!-- <div style="text-align: center;">
  <img src="figures/cass/fig1.svg" width="75%" class="no-dark">
</div> -->

![](figures/cass/fig2.svg){fig-cap="" width=100% .no-dark}

:::
::: {.notes}

:::
---

::: {.editable}

![](figures/cass/fig3.svg){fig-cap="" height=100% .no-dark}

:::
::: {.notes}

:::

---

::: {.editable}
<!-- <div style="text-align: center;">
  <img src="figures/cass/fig1.svg" width="75%" class="no-dark">
</div> -->

![](figures/cass/fig5.svg){fig-cap="" height=100% .no-dark}

:::
::: {.notes}

:::


## Why This Matters: The Gap Between Research and Practice

**Lesson Learned:** Prospective studies reveal what retrospective studies miss

| Aspect | Retrospective | Prospective |
|--------|---------------|-------------|
| Data quality | Pre-existing, variable | Controlled, standardized |
| Timing | Fixed (events already occurred) | Real-time (can intervene) |
| Causal inference | Confounded | Can design for causality |
| Clinical relevance | Varies by hospital | Context-specific |
| Generalization | Hard to assess | Direct validation possible |

**Result:** Our prospective CASSANDRA study found that hybrid monitoring (ICU + GW wearables) significantly improves early detection of postoperative complications, which retrospective analyses failed to capture.

---

### Medical Event Data Standard (MEDS)

**Open Question:** *How can we enable reproducible ML research across healthcare systems without requiring every institution to engineer their own ecosystem?*

---

## The Infrastructure Crisis

**Problem Statement:** 

The ML4H community reinvents the wheel continuously:

- Different data extraction code for each hospital system
- Incompatible feature engineering approaches
- Non-standard task definitions
- Proprietary data formats prevent collaboration

**Example:** Mount Sinai Health System (MSHS) has 6.8 million patients
- **Before MEDS:** Data in OMOP format; every research project wrote custom extraction code (~3-6 weeks per project)
- **After MEDS:** Standardized ETL pipeline; research ready in days

**Vision:** Build infrastructure so researchers focus on science, not data engineering.

---

## The MEDS Framework

**Design Principles:**

1. **ML-First** - Optimized for ML tasks, not observational research
2. **Flexible** - Works with OMOP, proprietary EHRs, wearables
3. **Event-Based** - Natural representation for longitudinal healthcare data
4. **Interoperable** - Tools compose together without data duplication

**Core Schema (v0.4.0):**

```python
from pyarrow.schema import Schema as PyArrowSchema
import pyarrow as pa
class DataSchema(PyArrowSchema):
    subject_id: Required(pa.int64(), nullable=False)
    time: Required(pa.timestamp("us"), nullable=True)
    code: Required(pa.string(), nullable=False)
    numeric_value: Optional(pa.float32())
    text_value: Optional(pa.large_string())
```

**Advantage:** Single standard enables tool reuse across institutions [^[@medsworkinggroupMedicalEventData2024]].

---

## MEDS Adoption: Current Status


::::

**Ecosystem Tools:**


---

## MEDS-Inspect: Bridging Data Understanding Gap

**Problem:** Researchers don't understand the data they work with

**Solution:** Interactive web-based data exploration tool

Features:
- Histogram of measurement frequencies
- Unique code counts per patient
- Subject timeline visualization
- Code search and filtering
- Vocabulary distribution analysis

**Real-World Impact (MSHS dataset):**
- Median patient has 1,240 unique codes
- 1% of codes represent ~80% of events (heavy-tailed distribution)
- Discovered data quality issues (e.g., erroneous dates) before modeling

---

## MEDS-DEV: Decentralized External Validation Framework

**Motivation:** Foundation models must generalize across institutions

**Design:**
- Define standardized tasks (disease onset prediction)
- Each site independently trains and evaluates on their own MEDS data
- Results aggregated without sharing raw data (privacy-preserving)

---

## Foundation Models for EHR Data

---

## MSHS Foundation Model Training

**Proof of Concept: Training on MSHS Dataset** 

**Dataset:**
- 6.8 million patients
- Tokenized to 39,000 most common medical codes
- ~2 billion events total

---

## Scaling Up Clinical ML from Datasets to Health Systems

![](figures/meds/mount_sinai/benchmarking_mshs.svg){fig-cap="" width=100% .no-dark}

---

## The Standardization Impact: From Theory to Practice

**Before MEDS:**
```
New Research Project
         ↓
    [Data Access Request]
         ↓
    [Custom ETL Code] (3-6 weeks)
         ↓
    [Feature Engineering] (2-4 weeks)
         ↓
    [Cohort Definition] (1-2 weeks)
         ↓
    [Actual ML Work] (weeks to months)
    
Total: 2-4 months before first model training
```

**After MEDS:**
```
New Research Project
         ↓
    [Use Existing ETL] (days)
         ↓
    [Task Definition] (1-2 days)
         ↓
    [Model Training + Evaluation] (days)
    
Total: 1-2 weeks before publication-ready results
```


---


## Ethical Considerations Across All Work

**Privacy:** 
- HIPAA, GDPR compliance in data collection
- MEDS-DEV designed for decentralized evaluation (data stays local)
- Wearable devices in CASSANDRA with explicit consent

**Fairness:**
- Demographic representation in training cohorts varies by institution
- Retrospective observational data contains historical biases in treatment decisions 
- Foundation models may amplify existing inequities if not carefully validated

**Transparency:**
- Published YAIB code enables reproducibility and scrutiny
- CASSANDRA registered prospective study (reduces publication bias)
- MEDS ecosystem enables open science practices
- 
<!--
---

 ## Synthesis: How Three Pillars Connect

**The Virtuous Cycle:**

```
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                                                      │
│  YAIB (Reproducible Benchmarks)                     │
│  ├─ Demonstrates importance of cohort/preprocessing │
│  ├─ Provides baselines for model comparison         │
│  └─ → Feeds into MEDS standardization               │
│                                                      │
│  CASSANDRA (Clinical Reality Check)                │
│  ├─ Shows ICU models fail in general ward          │
│  ├─ Demonstrates value of multimodal data          │
│  ├─ Validates prospective designs                  │
│  └─ → Informs MEDS feature requirements            │
│                                                      │
│  MEDS (Scalable Infrastructure)                    │
│  ├─ Enables YAIB-like benchmarks across sites      │
│  ├─ Makes prospective studies easier to conduct    │
│  ├─ Supports foundation model training at scale    │
│  └─ → Allows broader reproducibility validation    │
│                                                      │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

--- -->
---

## How Do We Create Research that Translates to Clinical Practice? 
::: {.bigger-text}
In *Data science at the singularity*, Donoho defines three characteristics that enable frictionless reproducibility^[[@donohoDataScienceSingularity2024]] : 

::: {.incremental}
- **Data**: Datafication of everything, with a culture of research data sharing. 
- **Re-execution**: Research code sharing including the ability to exactly re-execute the same complete  workflow by different researchers.  
- **Challenges**: Adopting challenge problems as a new paradigm powering methodological research.
:::
:::
<!-- > "Despite significant advances in machine learning capabilities, few AI systems have been successfully deployed into clinical practice. Key barriers include reproducibility challenges, generalization failures, and lack of standardized evaluation frameworks." -->

<!-- :::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Current State:**
- Limited open-source tools
- Dataset-specific implementations
- Proprietary data formats
- Few external validations
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Consequences:**
- Non-reproducible results
- Slow scientific progress
- Inefficient use of clinical data
- Barriers to adoption
:::
:::: -->

::: {.notes}
The translation gap in healthcare ML can be reframed as a failure to achieve the "data science singularity" as defined by Donoho. To reach this state, we need to ensure that data is widely available and standardized, that code is shared and can be re-executed by others, and that we adopt challenge problems to drive methodological research. My thesis addresses these needs through the development of tools and frameworks that promote reproducibility, clinical relevance, and scalable infrastructure for healthcare machine learning research.
:::

## Future Research Directions

**Immediate Priorities (1-2 years):**

1. **Causal Inference in EHR Data**
   - Move beyond pure prediction to understanding mechanism
   - Identify which data collection changes would improve outcomes
   - MEDS format enables efficient counterfactual analysis

2. **Cross-Institutional Model Development** 
   - Use MEDS-DEV to train models collaboratively
   - Federated learning approaches for privacy-preserving collaboration 
   - Benchmark against proprietary commercial systems

3. **Interpretability and Trust** 
   - Foundation models are black boxes—how do clinicians understand predictions?
   - SHAP analysis for clinical decision support
   - Prospective studies of clinician acceptance with interpretable models

**Longer-Term Vision (3-5 years):**

- **Multi-Modal Integration:** Seamless fusion of structured EHR + notes + imaging + wearables
- **Real-Time Deployment:** Predictive models integrated into EHR workflows (not separate systems)
- **Distributed Learning:** Hospitals train collaboratively without data sharing (privacy-preserving)

---

## United Nations WHO Digital Health Objectives

::: {.centered}
![](figures/motivation/WHO_logo.svg){fig-cap="World Health Organization" height=5em .no-dark}
:::

::: {.bigger-text}

::: {.fragment .fade-in fragment-index=1}

::: {.fragment .highlight-green fragment-index=5}
   - Promote global collaboration & advance the transfer of knowledge on digital health
:::
::: 
::: {.fragment .fade-in fragment-index=2}
   - Advance the implementation of national digital health strategies
:::
::: {.fragment .fade-in fragment-index=3}
   - Strengthen governance for digital health at global, regional and national levels
:::
::: {.fragment .fade-in fragment-index=4}
   - Advocate people-centered health systems that are enabled by digital health
:::

:::
::: {.notes}
In my thesis we focus on 1 in particularly while respecting and complementing the other objectives; of which the latter 3 and 4 are most important.
- https://www.itu.int/en/ITU-T/Workshops-and-Seminars/2024/0509/Documents/Carl%20LEITNER.pdf 
:::


## Central Argument

The translation gap in ML4H cannot be solved by better algorithms. We require:

1. **Transparent benchmarking frameworks** (YAIB) to understand what actually drives model performance
2. **Prospective clinical studies** (CASSANDRA) to improve quality and validate assumptions from retrospective research
3. **Standardized infrastructure** (MEDS) to enable collaborative, reproducible research at scale


**The Thesis Summarized:**
*Healthcare ML requires not just better models, but better infrastructure, validation practices, and institutional commitment to reproducibility.*

---

## Closing Remarks

**Looking Forward:**

<!-- The field of machine learning for healthcare stands at an inflection point. We have:
- ✓ Demonstrated predictive capability (models work in controlled settings)
- ✓ Shown deployment is feasible (CASSANDRA, clinical partnerships)
- ✓ Built reusable infrastructure (MEDS ecosystem)

**What Remains:**
- Translating research into routine clinical practice (workflow integration)
- Ensuring equitable deployment across diverse healthcare systems
- Maintaining humility about what ML can and cannot do in healthcare -->

---

<!-- ## Thank You -->

<!-- **Questions?** -->

<!-- *Key Takeaways:*
1. Reproducibility framework (YAIB) reveals that experimental setup often matters more than model choice
2. Prospective validation (CASSANDRA) shows what works in reality, not just in retrospective analysis
3. Standardized data format (MEDS) enables community collaboration and faster scientific progress -->

<!-- *Broader Vision:*
Healthcare ML can improve outcomes when built on solid infrastructure, rigorous validation, and commitment to reproducibility. -->


## References

::: {#refs}
:::

<!-- {{< include extra_slides/publications.qmd >}} -->
